attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two week history of severe vomiting. She is eight weeks pregnant and for the last two weeks has been vomiting 10 times a day and dry heaving regularly. She is vomiting whatever she has eaten or drunk and has not noticed any blood in her vomit. She has no fever, abdominal pain, and has no sick contacts. Her clothes now feel too big and she suspects she has lost weight in the last two weeks. She is concerned that all the vomiting is harming her baby.

On examination, her pulse rate is 110/min and blood pressure 124/80mmHg. Her eyes appear sunken. Blood tests reveal mild hyponatraemia and hypokalaemia. An obstetric ultrasound confirms a live intrauterine pregnancy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management at this stage?
options: Arrange for admission to receive IV fluids and monitoring
Give antiemetic and trial of food in Emergency
Send home with antiemetic medication and see GP with 48 hours
Send home with antiemetic medication and see GP with 7 days
attributes.explanation:
This woman is ill. She is not able to tolerate anything by mouth, is losing weight and to try and resolve this on an outpatient basis would almost be negligent. She needs to be admitted for intravenous fluid replacement, antiemetics and close monitoring. She has presented with hyperemesis gravidum (HG) with electrolyte disturbance and therefore requires admission to hospital for rehydration with IV fluids and electrolyte correction. It would also be appropriate to consider parenteral antiemetics, thiamine and a dietician review if there is prolonged poor intake of food. Venous thromboembolic (VTE) prophylaxis as an inpatient should also be given as an inpatient (for example, with 40mg enoxaparin subcutaneous daily). After a period of rehydration and stabilisation it may be suitable to consider ongoing management in a hospital in the home or outpatient setting.

Give antiemetic and trial of food in Emergency is incorrect. While it is important to control the nausea and vomiting, this patient needs urgent correction of her dehydration. It would be appropriate to trial food following successful rehydration with antiemetics if needed.

Send home with antiemetic medication and see GP with 48 hours is incorrect. This patient is presenting with electrolyte disturbances as a result of her vomiting and dehydration. It would be inappropriate to send this patient home. However, as is always the case, it would be important to involve this patient's GP as she may need ongoing management of nausea and vomiting once she is sent home.

Send home with antiemetic medication and see GP with 7 days is incorrect. This patient is presenting with electrolyte disturbances as a result of her vomiting and dehydration. It would be inappropriate to send this patient home. However, as is always the case, it would be important to involve the GP in this patient's care as she may need ongoing management of nausea and vomiting once she is sent home.

Send home with antiemetic medication is incorrect. This patient is presenting with electrolyte disturbances as a result of her vomiting and dehydration. This needs to be corrected before she is sent home.
learningPoints: Patients presenting with hyperemesis gravidarum (HG) with severe dehydration and electrolyte disturbances should be admitted to hospital for management.
After a period of rehydration and stabilisation it may be suitable to consider ongoing management of hyperemesis in a hospital in the home (if offered by the hospital) or outpatient setting.
references: Lowe S, Armstrong G, Beech A, Bowyer L, Grzeskowiak L, Marnoch C et al. SOMANZ position paper on the management of nausea and vomiting in pregnancy and hyperemesis gravidarum. Australian and New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology. 2019;60(1):34-43.
